cards.title: Adult Basic Life Support (BLS) — Algorithm
cards._legacyHtml:
🚨 UNRESPONSIVE PATIENT — START HERE
Check safety → Shout for help → Check breathing (look, listen, feel ≤10 sec)
1
No breathing / only gasping? → Call emergency services (108) immediately. Get AED if available.
2
Start CPR: 30 chest compressions : 2 rescue breaths. Rate: 100–120/min. Depth: 5–6 cm. Allow full chest recoil. Minimise interruptions.
3
AED arrives: Power on → attach pads → follow voice prompts. Analyse rhythm → shock if advised → immediately resume CPR for 2 min.
4
Continue 30:2 cycles. Reassess every 2 minutes. Do NOT stop for more than 10 seconds. Switch compressor every 2 min to avoid fatigue.
5
Signs of ROSC? (Return Of Spontaneous Circulation) — Normal breathing, coughing, movement → stop CPR → recovery position if not indicated otherwise → continue monitoring.
🟡 Key CPR quality points
Push HARD (5–6 cm) and FAST (100–120/min). Allow complete chest recoil between compressions. Minimise interruptions — target pre-shock pause <5 seconds. If no CPR training: compression-only CPR is acceptable and better than nothing. Use heel of hand, interlocked fingers, arms straight, directly over lower half of sternum.

cards.title: ACLS Shockable Rhythms — VF / Pulseless VT
cards._legacyHtml:
VF / Pulseless VT — SHOCKABLE
No pulse confirmed → CPR in progress → rhythm check shows VF or VT
StepActionDetail
1ShockBiphasic 120–200J (or monophasic 360J). Shout "All clear" before shock.
2Resume CPR immediately2 minutes — do NOT check pulse right after shock
3IV/IO accessAdrenaline 1mg IV/IO every 3–5 min (after 2nd shock)
4Rhythm check at 2 minIf still VF/VT → shock again → CPR → Amiodarone 300mg IV after 3rd shock
5Reversible causes4 H's + 4 T's — see below
4 H's + 4 T's — Reversible Causes
4 H's: Hypoxia · Hypo/Hyperkalaemia · Hypothermia · Hypovolaemia
4 T's: Tension pneumothorax · Tamponade · Toxins · Thrombosis (PE or coronary)

cards.title: ACLS Non-Shockable — PEA & Asystole
cards._legacyHtml:
PEA / Asystole — NON-SHOCKABLE
No pulse + flat line or organised rhythm without pulse
1
Continue CPR 30:2. Do NOT shock asystole — this will not help.
2
Adrenaline 1mg IV/IO as soon as IV access obtained. Repeat every 3–5 minutes throughout resuscitation.
3
Treat reversible causes — 4 H's + 4 T's. PEA is almost always caused by one of these. Think: tension pneumo, tamponade, hypovolaemia, hypoxia first.
4
Rhythm check every 2 min. If VF/VT develops → switch to shockable algorithm immediately.
🟡 Asystole — double-check leads
Before accepting asystole: check lead connections, increase gain, check in 2 leads. Fine VF can look like asystole — if in any doubt, attempt defibrillation. Atropine is no longer recommended in PEA/asystole (removed from 2015 AHA guidelines).

cards.title: ACLS Emergency Drugs — Quick Reference
cards._legacyHtml:
DrugIndicationDoseRouteNotes
Adrenaline (Epinephrine)Cardiac arrest (all rhythms), anaphylaxisArrest: 1mg every 3–5 min | Anaphylaxis: 0.5mg IM (anterolateral thigh)IV/IO (arrest), IM (anaphylaxis)First dose after 2nd rhythm check in VF/VT; immediately in PEA/asystole
AmiodaroneRefractory VF/pVT300mg IV bolus (first dose), 150mg (second dose)IV/IOAfter 3rd shock in shockable rhythm. Dilute in 5% dextrose 20ml.
AtropineSymptomatic bradycardia0.5mg IV every 3–5 min (max 3mg)IVNot for heart block (3rd degree) or transplanted heart. NOT in arrest.
AdenosineSVT (narrow complex regular tachycardia)6mg rapid IV push + flush → 12mg → 12mgIV (largest vein, fast push)Warn patient of transient chest pressure/flushing. Brief asystole is expected.
Magnesium sulphateTorsades de pointes, eclampsia, severe asthma2g IV over 10 min (TdP/asthma), 4g IV over 5–15 min (eclampsia)IVTdP: may need to repeat. Eclampsia: maintenance 1–2g/hr. Monitor reflexes.
Sodium bicarbonateTCA overdose, severe acidosis (pH <7.1), hyperkalaemia50mmol (50ml of 8.4%) IVIVNot routine in cardiac arrest. Check ABG. May worsen intracellular acidosis.
Calcium chlorideHyperkalaemia, hypocalcaemia, CCB overdose10ml of 10% CaCl2 IV slowlyIV (central preferred)Cardioprotective — does not lower K+, stabilises membrane. Given before bicarbonate in hyperkalaemia.

cards.title: Anaphylaxis — Recognition & Management
cards._legacyHtml:
ANAPHYLAXIS TRIAD — suspect if 2+ systems involved
Skin/mucosal + respiratory + cardiovascular, following exposure to allergen
1
Remove trigger — Stop offending drug/food. Call for help.
2
Adrenaline 0.5mg IM immediately — anterolateral mid-thigh. Can repeat after 5 min if no improvement. Do NOT delay for IV access.
3
Position: Supine (+ legs elevated if hypotensive) unless breathing difficulty (sit up). Pregnant — left lateral tilt.
4
Oxygen high-flow (15L/min via non-rebreather mask). IV access + fluid resuscitation (500ml–1L crystalloid bolus if hypotensive).
5
Adjuncts (after adrenaline): Chlorphenamine 10mg IM/IV (antihistamine) + Hydrocortisone 200mg IV (prevents biphasic). Salbutamol nebuliser if bronchospasm persists.
6
Observe minimum 6 hours — biphasic anaphylaxis in 5–20%. Admit if severe or refractory. Prescribe adrenaline auto-injector (EpiPen) on discharge.
🟡 Adrenaline dosing reminder
0.5mg IM = 0.5ml of 1:1000 adrenaline. Auto-injectors (EpiPen adult) deliver 0.3mg. Children: 0.01mg/kg IM (max 0.5mg). Adrenaline is the ONLY first-line drug — antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts ONLY and must never replace or delay adrenaline.

cards.title: Shock — Classification & Recognition
cards._legacyHtml:
TypeMechanismCausesKey FeaturesFirst step
Hypovolaemic↓ Circulating volumeHaemorrhage ★, burns, dehydration, diarrhoea/vomitingCool clammy, ↑HR, ↓BP, ↓JVP, flat neck veinsIV fluids + stop bleeding
Cardiogenic↓ Cardiac output (pump failure)MI ★, arrhythmia, PE, myocarditis, valvular emergencyCool clammy, ↑HR, ↓BP, ↑JVP, pulmonary oedema (crackles)Treat cause; diuresis + inotropes
Distributive — Septic↓ SVR (vasodilation + maldistribution)Infection → SIRS → sepsis ★WARM peripheries (early), ↑HR, ↓BP, ↓SVR, high CO earlyIV fluids + broad-spectrum ABx within 1hr
Distributive — Anaphylactic↓ SVR + capillary leakAllergen exposureUrticaria, angioedema, stridor/wheeze, warm skinAdrenaline 0.5mg IM IMMEDIATELY
ObstructiveMechanical obstruction to flowTension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, massive PE↑JVP, hypotension, ↓breath sounds unilaterally (tension) or muffled heart sounds (tamponade)Decompress immediately (needle thoracocentesis/pericardiocentesis)
NeurogenicLoss of sympathetic toneSpinal cord injury (above T6)Bradycardia + hypotension (no compensatory tachycardia), warm dry skin below lesionIV fluids + vasopressors (noradrenaline)
🟡 SHOCK mnemonic — early warning
S — Skin (pale, mottled, clammy) · H — Heart rate (>100/min) · O — Output (urine <0.5 ml/kg/hr) · C — Capillary refill (>2 seconds) · K — Known cause. Early shock = compensated (BP still normal). Do not wait for hypotension — treat the cause immediately.

cards.title: Sepsis — Sepsis-3 Criteria & Bundles
cards._legacyHtml:
Sepsis definition
Life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response to infection
SOFA score ≥2
Change from baseline = organ dysfunction
qSOFA (bedside)
RR ≥22 · Altered mentation · SBP ≤100
Septic shock
Sepsis + vasopressors needed + lactate >2 mmol/L despite fluids
Sepsis-1 Hour Bundle (SCCM/ESICM 2018)
1. Measure lactate (repeat if >2 mmol/L)
2. Blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics (2 sets)
3. Broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour
4. 30ml/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
5. Vasopressors (noradrenaline) if MAP <65 during/after fluids
🟡 Lactate — the metabolic fire alarm
Lactate >2 mmol/L = tissue hypoperfusion even with normal BP. Lactate >4 mmol/L = 40% mortality. Serial lactate normalisation (>10% clearance in 2hr) is the target. Causes of elevated lactate in sepsis: tissue hypoperfusion, mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired clearance. Lactate-guided resuscitation reduces mortality. Request it on every sick patient.

cards.title: Respiratory Emergencies — Quick Guide
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionKey SignsImmediate Action
Tension pneumothoraxTracheal deviation away, absent breath sounds unilaterally, ↑JVP, ↓BP, respiratory distressNeedle decompression: 2nd ICS, MCL (or 4th/5th ICS, AAL). Do NOT wait for X-ray. Followed by formal chest drain.
Acute severe asthmaPEFR 33–50%, RR>25, HR>110, SpO2<92%, cannot complete sentencesSalbutamol 5mg neb back-to-back, O2, ipratropium 0.5mg neb, hydrocortisone 100mg IV, MgSO4 2g IV. ICU if life-threatening features.
Pulmonary oedema (acute)Orthopnoea, frothy pink sputum, bilateral basal crackles, ↑JVP, ↓SpO2Sit upright, O2, GTN spray/IV (SBP>110), furosemide 40–80mg IV, morphine 2.5mg IV (cautiously). CPAP if available.
Massive PESudden collapse, hypoxia, ↑HR, ↑JVP, pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, S1Q3T3 ECGO2, anticoagulation (LMWH/UFH), consider thrombolysis if haemodynamically unstable (SBP <90). Surgical/catheter embolectomy in refractory cases.

cards.title: Acute Coronary Syndrome — STEMI Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Suspected ACS — Act within minutes
Chest pain/equivalent + ECG changes — time is muscle
1
MONA — Morphine (pain, carefully), Oxygen (only if SpO2 <94%), Nitrates (GTN spray unless SBP <90 or RV infarct), Aspirin 300mg chewed immediately.
2
12-lead ECG within 10 min. ST elevation ≥1mm in 2 contiguous limb leads, or ≥2mm in contiguous chest leads = STEMI. New LBBB = treat as STEMI.
3
Reperfusion decision: Primary PCI is gold standard (door-to-balloon <90 min). If PCI unavailable within 120 min: thrombolysis (streptokinase or tPA) if no contraindications.
4
Dual antiplatelet: Aspirin 300mg + Ticagrelor 180mg (or Clopidogrel 300–600mg if ticagrelor unavailable). Anticoagulation: LMWH or UFH.
5
Statin, beta-blocker, ACEi — start within 24 hours unless contraindicated. Admit to CCU for monitoring.
🟡 Posterior MI — the hidden STEMI
Posterior STEMI shows as ST depression + tall R waves in V1-V3 (mirror image of inferior elevation). Do posterior leads (V7, V8, V9 — left posterior chest). Also check right-sided leads (V3R, V4R) to detect RV infarction — critical because RV MI contraindicates nitrates and requires IV fluids rather than diuretics.
